coredata.dc:description: Commercializing emerging photovoltaic (PV) technologies is hindered by challenges such as performance instability, short lifespans, and reliance on expensive, non-eco-friendly materials. Key contributors to PV degradation and instability include ultraviolet (UV) radiation and environmental factors like humidity, air, and temperature. While UV-related issues can be mitigated through luminescent downshifting (LDS), environmental hazards are typically addressed through encapsulation. The industry standard, ethylene–vinyl acetate (EVA), used for encapsulating and laminating solar cells, is both expensive and toxic, and lacks effective UV protection. To overcome these challenges, an environmentally friendly, cost-effective LDS nanocomposite based on carbon dots (CDs) and epoxy was developed in this study, which can be applied as a laminating layer for PVs. This nanocomposite efficiently converts ultraviolet photons into visible light, enhancing the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of silicon solar cells by 1.65% and achieving a current density gain of 8.33 mAcm−2. The synthesized carbon dots exhibit a photoluminescence quantum yield of 34%. The resulting PCE improvement is significant, as it could lead to a bill of materials (BoM) cost reduction of approximately 1.58% for monocrystalline silicon PV modules, factoring in the cost of nanocomposite application. This translates to a cost reduction from $0.10/watt to $0.098418/watt. This innovative nanocomposite not only provides LDS functionality but also serves as a robust, industrially scalable encapsulant, offering a green and economically viable solution for improving the stability and performance of PV devices and other optoelectronic applications.

head.ce:title: Improvement in solar cells efficiency with luminescent downshifting nanocomposite lamination: A green alternative for ethylene–vinyl acetate

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Commercializing emerging photovoltaic (PV) technologies is hindered by challenges such as performance instability, short lifespans, and reliance on expensive, non-eco-friendly materials. Key contributors to PV degradation and instability include ultraviolet (UV) radiation and environmental factors like humidity, air, and temperature. While UV-related issues can be mitigated through luminescent downshifting (LDS), environmental hazards are typically addressed through encapsulation. The industry standard, ethylene–vinyl acetate (EVA), used for encapsulating and laminating solar cells, is both expensive and toxic, and lacks effective UV protection. To overcome these challenges, an environmentally friendly, cost-effective LDS nanocomposite based on carbon dots (CDs) and epoxy was developed in this study, which can be applied as a laminating layer for PVs. This nanocomposite efficiently converts ultraviolet photons into visible light, enhancing the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of silicon solar cells by 1.65% and achieving a current density gain of 8.33 mAcm−2. The synthesized carbon dots exhibit a photoluminescence quantum yield of 34%. The resulting PCE improvement is significant, as it could lead to a bill of materials (BoM) cost reduction of approximately 1.58% for monocrystalline silicon PV modules, factoring in the cost of nanocomposite application. This translates to a cost reduction from $0.10/watt to $0.098418/watt. This innovative nanocomposite not only provides LDS functionality but also serves as a robust, industrially scalable encapsulant, offering a green and economically viable solution for improving the stability and performance of PV devices and other optoelectronic applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Most of the world's grid energy comes from nonrenewable sources such as oil, gas, coal, biofuels, and industrial waste. The production of energy from nonrenewable sources and CO2 emissions are interlinked and need to be addressed. To deal with the carbon emission-associated global warming and meet the increasing energy demands, appropriate utilization of renewable energy resources is extremely important [1]. Renewable energy resources like solar, hydel, wind, geothermal and tidal can play a key role in combating global warming and meeting the ever-increasing global energy needs. Most of these resources are climate dependent which adversely affects their feasibility [2]. Among these, solar energy is the most abundant, sustainable, and green. A photovoltaic (PV) cell is a device which directly converts solar energy into electricity. It has a great market value due to its cost-effectiveness and easy implementation on small and large scale [3]. According to Global Off-Grid Lighting Association (GOGLA), 7.6 million off-grid solar products having a capacity of 59 MW were sold in 2018 amounting to $483 million [4]. Fortune Business Insights claims that the global solar power market is expected to grow from $184.03 billion in 2021 to $293.18 billion in 2028. The conventional Si wafer-based solar modules have led to amazing price reductions as a result of advancements in the relevant technology. Despite these advantages, Si PVs could not gain significant market traction owing to their fundamental disadvantages, such as disposability, high temperature processing and form factor [5,6]. While translating PV technology from laboratory to market, minimum cost, maximum power conversion efficiency and long lifetime are the three benchmarks in addition to other factors like payback period and disposability [15].

Recently, metal-halide Perovskite Solar Cells (PSCs) emerged as a new technology in PVs with excellent electro-optic properties like high absorption coefficients [7], high and balanced charge carrier mobility [8], tunable bandgap [9], long intrinsic lifetime [10], long diffusion lengths (0.1–1.0 mm), impressive power conversion efficiency (PCE∼23 %), low cost and facile fabrication [11,12]. Since 2009, efforts have been made to establish perovskite solar cells (PSCs) as one of the promising PV technologies [13,14] but could not make its due market. The main reasons for this failure include their poor stability and durability which are mainly associated with the ultraviolet (UV) radiation and environmental dynamics [15,16]. So far, the longest lifetime of around one year has been reported for PSCs which is much shorter than the required life span (at least 25 years) for their commercialization [17]. For this purpose, studies regarding the stability and efficiency of the emerging and already developed PV technologies are needed [18,19]. The factors influencing the durability and lifespan of a solar cell may be extrinsic and / or intrinsic. Extrinsic factors such as humidity and oxidation can be easily addressed via encapsulation of these cells, but the intrinsic issues are relatively more difficult to deal with. These issues come from the intrinsic instability of the constituent materials, interfaces between layers and the charge transport / carrier materials [15]. The main intrinsic factors leading to instability include thermal instability, ion migration and UV degradation [20]. Various techniques have been used to mitigate the intrinsic instability of solar cells, e.g., via replacement/substitution of selective contacts [21,22], interlayer insertions [23,24], development of novel cells and module configurations [25,26], modification of the light-absorbing materials [27,28] and luminescent downshifting (LDS) [29–31]. Among these, LDS has been reported to be the most efficient and cost-effective option [29–31]. LDS is an optical approach based on shifting the high energy photons from the UV region to the visible region. It was first proposed by Hovel and coworkers in 1979 [32]. For emerging PVs, the LDS phenomenon was first demonstrated by Leijtens et al, [33] in 2013. So far, lanthanide complexes, quantum dots (QDs), dyes, nanophosphors, rare-earths and some non-rare-earth elements have also been used as LDS materials for solar cells [34–40]. The common methods used for introducing LDS mechanism in solar cell technology include coatings, encapsulation, and incorporation of LDS over or inside the solar cell structure [36–39]. The process of incorporation of LDS inside the structure of a solar cell is complicated and costly [36–39]. Furthermore, such mechanisms offer partial solution and need modifications with variations in the solar cell architecture [41]. To overcome this problem, inclusion of LDS layer in a solar cell cover-glass has been reported for enhancing the stability and lifetime of PVs without any compromise on the solar cell architecture [42,43]. One of the major disadvantages of LDS cover-glass is the use of toxic and expensive materials, commonly rare earths, and lead (Pb) containing compounds [42,43]. Keeping in view the above-mentioned limitations of the available LDS materials along with the difficulties in their disposability and fabrication methods, a novel, green and a cost-effecti ve LDS approach has been developed in the present study. For this purpose, a nanocomposite coating was developed to laminate the solar cell and its cover-glass. Carbon Dots (CDs) and epoxy were used to synthesize the LDS nanocomposite. CDs constitute a new class of fluorescent carbonaceous nanomaterials different from the other carbon allotropes (such as fullerenes, graphene, semiconductor CDs, quantum dots and carbon nanotubes), with remarkable properties like easy synthesis, minimum cost, high biocompatibility, excellent solubility, and tunable fluorescence [44]. Furthermore, CDs can be easily engineered for multiple applications [44–51]. Epoxy was used as a polymeric matrix to prepare LDS nanocomposite because of its minimum cost, environmental friendliness, room temperature processibility, easy recyclability and almost 80–85 % transparency with UV cut-off at 300 nm [52]. Additionally, epoxy is relatively more flexible and can sustain high pressures along with self-healing properties [52,53]. The LDS nanocomposite has been simplified and can be easily applied through coating onto any kind of solar cell cover-glass at room temperature.

Thus, in the present study, non-toxic and rare-earth-free CDs, exhibiting LDS behavior with a quantum yield of 34 %, were synthesized in a domestic Microwave (MW) oven through a MW assisted technique [54–64]. A controllable quantity of CDs was blended in a transparent epoxy glue by magnetic stirring and sonication. LDS nanocomposite was spin-coated over a cover-glass slide and the nanocoated face of the glass and solar cell were hot pressed together. This nanocomposite could not only provide the required encapsulation and lamination but also efficiently convert the hazardous high energy UV photons into useful visible photons. This increased the incident photons current efficiency (IPCE) of a Si PV cell (BPW34) by 1.65 % with a current density gain of 8.33 mAcm−2. The resulting PCE improvement is significant, as it could lead to a bill of materials (BoM) cost reduction of approximately 1.58 % for monocrystalline silicon PV modules, factoring in the cost of nanocomposite application. This translates to a cost reduction from $0.10/watt to $0.098418/watt.

Citric acid (C6H8O7) with ≥ 99.5 % purity (MW 192.12 g/mol), urea (CH4N2O) with 99 % purity (MW 60.06 g/mol), cellulose dialyzers 16000–8000 MW Cut-Off (MWCO) 16–8 Kda, quinine hemi-sulfate salt monohydrate (purity 99 %) and sulfuric acid were acquired from Sigma Aldrich (USA). A highly transparent epoxy resin and a miniaturized solar cell BPW34 photodiode (a silicon PIN photodiode with a radiant sensitive area of 7.5 mm2) manufactured by Vishay Inter technology, Inc. (USA) were acquired from the local market. Its small size, easy availability and low cost make it ideal for its use in research studies as reference solar cells, and also in PV applications, light detection, optical communication, and remote sensing. It has a spectral response range of 350 to 1100 nm. Additionally, BPW34 has a low dark current which helps in reducing noise in the detection circuit. MW assisted synthesis of CDs was carried out using citric acid and urea through a method reported in literature [65–68] with a few modifications. The chemical reaction of urea with citric acid at 1:1 ratio in aqueous solution yields urea citrate monohydrate (C7H6N2O8·H2O) crystals with a maximum nitrification of 11.1 wt% [67]. The molecular fluorophore that forms during the synthesis of green CDs is 4-hydroxy-1H-pyrrolo [3,4-c] pyridine-1,3,6 (2H,5H)-trione (HPPT) [68]. The yield of the used method and experimental parameters (time and temperature) were optimized to check the suitability of the process for mass scale production. The chemical reaction between urea and citric acid in aqueous solution is given in Supplementary Information [hereafter, referred to as (SI)] S1 Figure 1. To synthesize CDs exhibiting green fluorescence, urea and citric acid (1:1 wt ratio 0.1 g each corresponding to the 3.2:1 M ratio) were dissolved in 1 ml of deionized (DI) water. 5 samples were prepared at the same concentration in flat-bottom transparent soda-lime silicate (SLS) glass bottles. The samples were crystallized on a hot plate at 40 °C. The resulting samples were processed under the open vessel conditions in a 0.7 KW domestic MW oven (on medium intensity for which operating power typically falls between 400–500 Watt) for 1 to 5 min. After each minute, one sample was taken out of the MW chamber and labeled as Sample# 1–5 and stored in a desiccator for further processing and characterization. After 5 min, the last sample was taken out. Each precipitate of CDs was diluted in 20 ml DI water. The diluted solution was sonicated and stirred for 10 min. Finally, uniformly mixed dark brown solutions of CDs were obtained. These solutions emitted in green light when illuminated with UV light (398 nm) which indicated the formation of CDs. The observation of the relatively darker color of each batch with an increase in processing time demonstrated the effect of longer reaction time on the concentration of CDs [67]. The brownish mixtures were dissolved in water and filtered with PVDF 0.1-µm syringe filter.

Further purification was carried out using dialysis bags. Dialysis is considered a crucial step and an efficient procedure in the synthesis of CDs and characterization of molecular fluorophores [68]. The entire synthesis and purification process of green CDs is schematically shown in Fig. 1 a and 1b, respectively. Fig. 1c shows the effect of CDs-based LDS glass and glass without CDs when illuminated with UV light. The real time representation of the glass visually confirmed the LDS phenomenon while the glass was fully transparent under the visible light.

The synthesized CDs and epoxy resin were mixed by magnetic stirring and then spin coated over a SLS glass slide. A thin sheet of CDs-based LDS polymeric glass was fixed over a BPW34 Si solar cell via thermal pressing at 120 °C.

Quantum efficiency, power conversion efficiency and current density of the solar cell were measured with and without LDS cover-glass to compare their spectral response and power conversion efficiency.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded using a XPert3 Powder X-ray diffractometer (Malvern Panalytical, Netherland) with a Cu Kαsource (λ=1.5418740 Å). The morphology and distribution of CDs were examined by a transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan). Photoluminescence emission (PLE) spectra were recorded at various excitation wavelengths using a Luminescence Spectrometer (LS45, Perkin Elmer, USA). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were recorded with a Lambda 1050 UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was carried out using a SpectrumTwo FTIR Spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). Current-voltage and power characteristics of a solar cell (BPW34 Photodiode) were measured with a 40 × 40 mm AAA class solar simulator along with a certified mono-crystalline silicon 20 × 20 mm calibrated reference cell using a Keithley 2401 source meter (SAN-EI ELECTRIC, Japan). Quantum efficiency and spectral response were measured with a PVE300 dual light source (Xenon-70 Watt and Quartz Halogen-100 Watt) EQE (IPCE)/IQE measurement system (Bentham, UK). Cost analysis of the materials acquired for the present study is given in Table 1 in SI S2.

During synthesis, urea and citric acid undergo thermal decomposition and carbonization. This process leads to the formation of carbon dots which are small carbon-based nanoparticles. The carbonization process typically involves the formation of a carbonaceous core surrounded by various functional groups derived from the starting materials. The FTIR spectra of CDs indicated the presence of O-H, C=O, C=C, C-H and C-O functional groups on the sample's surface. The broad bands observed between 2700 and 3600 cm−1 indicated the presence of O-H, N-H and C-H stretching vibrations whereas the observed broadening of these bands indicated strong H-bonding within the samples.

The appearance of C-H stretching vibrations in the IR spectrum at 3040 cm−1 and 2781 cm−1 is due to the presence of hydrogen atoms bonded to carbon atoms in the sample. During carbonization, the citric acid can decompose to form carbon–carbon (C-C) bonds and carbon-hydrogen (C-H) bonds. The presence of C-H bonds is a natural outcome of the carbonization process, as citric acid itself contains hydrogen atoms bonded to carbon atoms. The strong signals at 1562 and 1652 cm−1 may be assigned to the C=C and C=N bending/stretching modes, respectively. The band observed at 1393 cm−1 may be assigned to the C–N and C-O stretching/bending modes as reported in literature [68–70]. In general, the dominant bands observed on these FTIR spectra were identical with those reported [70] for urea citrate (given in SI S3 Figure 3). Thus, the present FTIR analysis confirmed the formation of CDs fluorophores with a rich gallery of carbon. Fig. 2 a shows FTIR spectra of the supernatant sol of sample #3 CDs directly dropped onto the diamond window recorded in the attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode.

The single broad XRD peak (Fig. 3 a) observed at 2θ ≈ 26.29° (matching JCPDS No. 41–1487 for graphite) may be attributed to the crystalline C species with a high degree of C (002) planes (d002 = 0.34 nm). This result reconfirmed the presence of amorphous carbon phase [71,72] with a well-ordered lamellar structure having layers' regularity [57]. The crystallite-size D of the CDs was calculated using Scherrer’s formula given by equation 1 [84].
(1)
D =
K λ
β c o s θ
where β is the full width at half maximum (radian), λ is the X-ray wavelength (Å), and θ is the Bragg diffraction angle of the XRD peak (degree). The crystallite size calculated from Scherrer’s formula is ∼ 16.37 nm.

The presence of the (002) peak also indicated that the interlayer stacking within CDs was very close to that of graphite (≈ 0.35 nm). This result further demonstrated that the close packed (0.34 nm) highly conjugated sp2 domains had a dominant place within the core of CDs. While on the edges of the CDs, the functional groups or sp3 C increased the interlayer distance [71]. The TEM image shown in Fig. 4 b indicated that the CDs had almost spherical or spheroidal morphology with dimensions ranging from 3 to 16 nm (inset in Fig. 3b), resembling the pore size of the dialysis membrane i.e., 8–16 Kda.

Analysis of UV–visible absorption and florescence spectra revealed that the main absorption bands were observed at ∼ 336 and 399 nm (Fig. 4a) and strong fluorescence at λex = 390 nm in the green region (λem = 540 nm) for each CDs sample (Fig. 4b). The UV–visible (blue line) and fluorescence emission spectra (green line) of the prepared CDs can be seen in Fig. 4b. Usually, emission at 540 nm at the excitation wavelength of 400 nm is associated with the CDs derived from urea and citric acid as reported in literature [68]. The first absorbance peak at 336 nm is related to the σ − σ∗ or π − π∗ transition in CDs. Both the σ − σ∗ and π − π∗ transitions come from the carbonic core centers whereas the last peak at 399 nm is related to the σ − π∗ transition, originating from the surface of CDs [73–75]. Thus, the absorption phenomena in CDs are associated with both the core and surface while the emission is strongly dependent on the surface state and size [76]. The high energy tail in the visible region of the absorption spectrum of CDs may be attributed to Mie scattering, caused by nano-sized particles [77]. Furthermore, when the excitation wavelength was increased from 400 to 480 nm, the fluorescence emission peak at 540 nm remained unchanged which showed the excitation-independent behavior of the synthesized CDs. The excitation-independent fluorescence emission of CDs is shown in Fig. 4c. Such a photoluminescence (PL) behavior may be related to a reduction in surface defects and uniformity in the size of CDs. It is also important to note that the absorption and optical properties of the synthesized CDs are almost identical to those reported earlier for highly homogeneous CDs obtained from citric acid and urea [68]. Further, The PL behavior at longer excitation wavelengths can be attributed to several factors. Firstly, the quantum confinement effect may result in smaller CD sizes as excitation wavelengths increase, potentially leading to decreased photoluminescence (PL) emission intensity. This reduction in emission intensity is primarily due to the lower number of excited states available for radiative recombination, resulting from the smaller size of CDs caused by the quantum confinement effect. Secondly, alterations in excitation wavelength can affect the bandgap transition of CDs, with longer wavelengths potentially exciting higher energy states and causing emission spectrum shifts towards shorter wavelengths. Additionally, variations in excitation wavelength can influence the surface states of CDs by interacting with different surface functional groups, thereby altering recombination pathways of excited states and impacting PL emission. Lastly, changes in excitation wavelength may also modify the interaction between CDs and surrounding solvent molecules, affecting solvation dynamics and resulting in fluctuations in PL emission intensity [83].

Fig. 4d shows the PL spectra of 5 samples treated sequentially for 1 to 5 min duration in a MW oven, labeled as S1, S2, S3, S4 and S5. The observed slight stock shift in emission with respect to the variation of MW processing time may be attributed to the emission from the core energy transition which is slightly influenced by the CDs concentration [78].

The procedure for measuring the quantitative yield and concentration of the Carbon Dots (CDs) is detailed in Supplementary Information (SI) Section 4. Similarly, the absorbance peaks of all the 5 samples were almost at the same wavelength with slight variations in the absorbance with increasing processing time, which can be seen in Fig. 4a. These variations may be associated with the increasing concentration of CDs per unit volume of the solution and this phenomenon is associated with the Beer’s Law. Slight variations in PL CIE color coordinates with change in MW processing time can be seen in Fig. 5 e as well. The slight stock shift towards the higher wavelength in the green region was prominent which confirmed the relation between the concentration of CDs and the MW processing time. Thus, the present spectroscopic study demonstrated the formation of high-quality CDs capable of shifting or absorbing the high energy photons and emitting low energy photons for their possible use in PVs for LDS application.

Excellent photoluminescence quantum yield (PL QY) is a key requirement for utilizing CDs in PVs. Two techniques are commonly used for QY measurements. One is the absolute method using integrating spheres and another is a quantitative method using a reference material of a known QY and concentration such as quinine sulfate. Although, the uncertainty in the latter technique is ± 10 % but it is widely used because of its low cost [79–81]. Additionally, optimal concentration selection is also crucial and critical for the measurement of quantitative QY of photoluminescent species [82]. In the present study, QY=0.34 of CDs was measured comparatively as shown in Fig. 5. For this purpose, quinine sulfate (QS) with a PL QY of 57 % and a refractive index of 1.33 was dissolved in 0.1 M solution of H2SO4. CDs were dissolved in water having the same refractive index (i.e., 1.33). Both the standard (QS) and the test sample (CD) exhibit maximum absorption at nearly 350 nm; therefore, the excitation wavelength was set to 350 nm for all samples when measuring fluorescence, at the same excitation wavelength can be assumed to be absorbing the same number of photons. To minimize re-absorption effects, the absorbance value in the 1 cm optical path length was kept below 0.10. For accurate measurement of QY the absorbance of samples with known and unknown QY were kept almost equal (ACD ≈ 0.025 and AQS ≈ 0.0245). Excitation and emission slit widths were fixed at 5 nm when recording their PL spectra. QY was calculated using equation (2)

where ø and I denote quantum yield (QY), measured integrated PLE intensity and A is the absorbance

Fig. 6 shows the current–voltage (IV) and power characteristics of a Si PV cell with and without a CDs-epoxy composite layer used as a luminescence downshifter. However, the mechanism of luminescence downshifting with and without the CDs-epoxy composite layer is schematically demonstrated in Figure 8 of the supplementary information. The PV performance parameters of the cell with both configurations are also summarized in Table 1. A comparison of the performance of these configurations demonstrated that with the inclusion of the CDs-epoxy composite layer, power conversion efficiency and current density of the under-test solar cells improved by 3.56 % and 0.833 mA/cm2, respectively.

Fig. 7 a shows the EQE response of the BPW34 Si solar cells with and without CDs cover-glass and percent change in EQE from λ1 = 315 nm to λ2 = 1095 nm. The LDS layer absorbed light of the shorter wavelengths (315–490 nm) and emitted visible light of the longer wavelength ≈ 540–550 nm where the spectral response of the Si solar cell was almost at maximum. The down-shifted photons reached the cell junction without parasitic absorption and thus, increased the EQE in the 315–490 nm spectral range (Region 1). The change in EQE was observed to be maximum at 400 nm which may be associated with the PL response of the CDs as shown in Fig. 4c (Section 3.3). The observed increase in EQE response in the 490–1095 nm spectral range (Region-2) indicated that the LDS layer acted as an anti-reflective coating as well [30]. After calculating the spectral response of each configuration between λ1 and λ2, the current density gain (ΔJsc) could be calculated using equation (2) [30].

where λ is the corresponding wavelength, q is the charge of the electron (1.60217662 × 10-19 C where 1 C = 1 As−1), and ΦAM1.5G (λ) is the photon flux according to the Astmg173 AM1.5G. Current density at each wavelength is associated with photon flux at the corresponding wavelength (Fig. 7b). The change in the integrated current density for each configuration (from λ1 = 315 nm to λ2 = 1095 nm) is almost equal to the change in current density calculated from the I-V characteristics of the solar cell under various configurations.

In addressing the challenges of environmental feasibility, efficiency, cost, stability, and disposability of photovoltaic (PV) technologies, a one-pot, one-step, environmentally friendly, cost-effective, and industrially scalable luminescent down-shifting (LDS) nanocomposite was developed in the present study. This LDS nanocomposite can be easily applied as a laminating layer over solar cells. Laminating silicon solar cells with this nanocomposite resulted in a significant enhancement of power conversion efficiency (PCE) by up to 1.65 %. The carbon dots (CDs) used in the synthesis of this nanocomposite film exhibited a quantum yield of 34 % and were synthesized via a straightforward one-pot microwave-assisted method. To ensure reproducibility and long-term stability, the experiment was repeated after eight months, with results confirming that the nanocomposite remained stable throughout this period. A comparison with previously reported PV LDS nanocomposites clearly demonstrates that the synthesized CDs nanocomposite presents a green, industrially scalable, and cost-effective solution for PV technologies and optoelectronic applications, including light-emitting devices. Furthermore, future work could focus on adjusting the emission spectrum of CDs to better match the solar cell's spectral sensitivity or increasing the quantum yield of CDs, potentially leading to further PCE improvements. The approach adopted in this study shows significant application potential for emerging PV technologies, such as perovskite solar panels, which are vulnerable to environmental hazards like UV light, moisture, and temperature. Additionally, this approach can be beneficial for improving the spectral response and mitigating the degradation of printable and flexible solar cells. In conclusion, the LDS nanocomposite synthesized in this study offers a cost-effective, green, and robust solution for mass-scale implementation in laminating and encapsulating PV devices, paving the way for enhanced performance and durability in next-generation solar technologies.
